text: During the simultaneous trans-anal transabdominal approach, trans-anal arthroscopy reveals a suspected fistula tract at the five o'clock position. The surgeon closes the fistula transanally using non-absorbable double figure-of-eight sutures, with no evident injury observed in the pelvic cavity during transabdominal inspection.

The anterior surface of the rectal stump is perforated using a mono-curved coagulation hook inserted transanally. A small silicone tube is temporarily inserted into the abdomen as part of the rendezvous approach. The coagulation hook is replaced with straight grasping forceps, and the silicone tube is passed through the rectal stump and out of the anus. The spike of a circular stapler is connected to the tube, and the stapler is introduced into the anus. The tube is removed, and the spike is connected to the anvil to create the anastomosis.

The surgeon inspects the repaired fistula located inferior to the anastomosis, which shows no defects. Contrast-enhanced ICG perfusion is applied to assess the anastomosis, which is positioned five centimeters from the pectineal line, before concluding the procedure with a protective ileostomy.

During the transanal revisional surgery, the surgeon identifies and removes previous intraluminal sutures from the anastomosis site. The defect is cleaned and brushed using a coagulating hook, followed by the introduction of new sutures through the D-port for repair.

Colonoscopy at four months showed a sinus fistula tract, which was not confirmed by NMR. After two months, the protective idea of STEMI is reversed, and at six months, the patient is doing well with the regular transit.
